title: Valero Texas Open — 2026 Tournament Preview
eventTag: PGA Tour • FedExCup • Last Chance for Augusta
venue: TPC San Antonio (Oaks Course), San Antonio, TX • Golf Channel / NBC
extraInfo: $9.8M purse • Par 72, 7,438 yards • Defending champion: Brian Harman • Winner earns Masters invitation • 500 FedExCup points

tournament.name: Valero Texas Open
tournament.courseName: TPC San Antonio (Oaks Course) • San Antonio, TX

stakes.callout: The Valero Texas Open is the last stop before Augusta National. The winner earns the final Masters invitation, and players like Rickie Fowler, Billy Horschel, Tony Finau, and Tom Kim are all fighting for that golden ticket. Tommy Fleetwood—the reigning FedExCup champion—headlines a field of 132 at TPC San Antonio's Oaks Course, a Greg Norman design where accuracy and iron play matter more than raw distance. Collin Morikawa withdrew on Monday with a lingering back injury, leaving the field wide open. Jordan Spieth returns to the site of his 2021 drought-breaking victory with the best course history of anyone in the field. And Thursday's forecast of 20+ mph winds and 84°F heat could make the opening rounds a survival test before a cold front drops temps to 59°F on Sunday.

cards.label: The Last Masters Ticket
cards.value: The Valero winner earns the final Masters invitation. Three of the last six Texas Open winners—Corey Conners (2019), J.J. Spaun (2022), and Akshay Bhatia (2024)—used this win to punch their ticket to Augusta. Rickie Fowler (World #65) needs to win outright. Tony Finau (World #107) hasn't had a top-10 in 13 months. Billy Horschel (World #94) is returning from injury. Tom Kim (World #126) is in a severe slump. The desperation is palpable.

cards.label: Morikawa Withdraws
cards.value: Collin Morikawa—one of the finest iron players on the planet—withdrew on March 31 with the same back injury that forced him out of The Players Championship after just one hole. His Masters participation is now in serious doubt. Morikawa's withdrawal removes a top-3 favorite and reshuffles the entire field. At a course where SG: Approach is the #1 predictor, losing perhaps the best approach player in the world is significant.

cards.label: FedExCup & Career Stakes
cards.value: The winner earns 500 FedExCup points, $1,764,000, and 50 OWGR points, plus invitations to the Masters, PGA Championship, The Sentry (2027), and remaining 2026 Signature Events. For Tommy Fleetwood, a win would be his first of 2026 despite being World #4. For Jordan Spieth, it would be his first victory since the 2022 RBC Heritage—and at the course where he broke his previous drought in 2021.

cards.label: Wild Weather Swing
cards.value: The forecast calls for 20+ mph winds and 84°F heat on Thursday and 88°F with gusty east winds on Friday—the two toughest scoring days. Then a cold front drops temperatures to 59°F on Sunday with calm winds. The delta between calm and windy winning scores at TPC San Antonio is roughly 10 strokes (−9 in windy 2025 vs. −20 in calm 2024). Wave advantage will be enormous.

styles.title: Precision Ball-Strikers Who Pick It Apart
bullets: SG: Approach is the #1 predictor at TPC San Antonio. Seven of the last eight winners ranked top 4 in SG: Approach during their winning week. Conners 2023 gained +2.385 strokes per round on approach; Bhatia 2024 gained +2.057; Harman 2025 gained +1.538. The GIR rate here is only 58%—tied with Riviera and Harbour Town as the toughest on Tour—so players who can find greens from imperfect lies separate themselves massively.

Driving accuracy matters more than distance. Fairways average only 25-30 yards wide—among the tightest on Tour. Only 56% of drives find fairways (vs. 62% Tour average). In four of the last five editions, the winner ranked 12th or better in Good Drives Gained. Recent winners averaged 26th in driving distance but much higher in accuracy. The oak-lined corridors on holes 4 (narrowest fairway) and 6 (200-yard carry) demand precision off the tee.

Bogey avoidance on the monster par 4s. In four of the last five editions, the winner ranked top 6 in bogey avoidance. Holes 1 (454 yds, 4.296 avg), 4 (481 yds, #1 HCP), 9 (474 yds, 29%+ over-par rate), and 15 (464 yds, #2 HCP) are all ranked among the top 50 toughest holes on Tour each year. Navigating the front nine without a big number is essential.

This profile fits: Henley, Spieth, Si Woo Kim, Conners, Noren. Russell Henley ranks 9th on Tour in bogey avoidance and 5th in driving accuracy—the #1 pick in multiple analytical models. Jordan Spieth has the best SG per round at TPC San Antonio of anyone in the field, "and it's not even close." Si Woo Kim is 2nd on Tour in SG: Approach, the single most predictive stat here.

styles.title: Short Game Wizards Who Grind
bullets: With only 58% of greens hit in regulation, scrambling opportunities are constant. In four of the last five editions, the winner ranked 24th or better in SG: Around the Green. The Champions Bermuda greens with short-grass runoffs require touch and imagination. Tommy Fleetwood leads the entire PGA Tour in SG: Around the Green at +0.900—the best short game on the planet.

Jordan Spieth won in 2021 with wedge play, not driving. He led the field in proximity from 50-125 yards at an astonishing 10'5" average—10+ feet better than the field. He led in birdie-or-better percentage and ranked 4th in scrambling. His path to victory was grinding out pars with his short game and capitalizing on scoring holes with precision wedges.

Par 5s reward wedge play, not power. Despite three par 5s over 591 yards, less than 45% of the field goes for the green in two. Par-5 GIR is only 11% (vs. 22% Tour average). The key differentiator is wedge play from 100 yards and in, not eagle putting. Hole 18 has played over par every year since 2010.

This profile fits: Fleetwood, Matsuyama, McCarthy. Fleetwood's elite short game (1st in SG: ATG) could overcome his poor approach numbers (97th). Hideki Matsuyama ranks top 40 in SG: ATG, scrambling, and sand saves. Denny McCarthy has a +1.82 SG per round at TPC San Antonio—2nd only to Spieth—and lost the 2024 playoff to Bhatia.

keys.title: The approach game decides everything. Seven of eight recent winners ranked top 4.
keys.description: TPC San Antonio is the PGA Tour's ultimate iron-play test. The GIR rate is only 58%—the toughest on Tour alongside Riviera and Harbour Town. Seven of the last eight winners ranked in the top 4 in SG: Approach during their winning week. Winner SG: Approach averages an extraordinary +1.708 per round. The narrow fairways (25-30 yards, only 56% hit) mean approach shots frequently come from imperfect lies in the rough, making the ability to still find greens from bad positions the ultimate separator. Players who rank outside the top 20 in approach for the week have almost no chance to win.

keys.title: Wind on Thursday/Friday could create a 3-stroke wave advantage.
keys.description: Sustained 20+ mph winds with gusts to 25 mph are forecast for Thursday and Friday. In 2015, the AM/PM scoring differential at TPC San Antonio was 3.9 strokes—the largest wave split on Tour that year. The course's design amplifies wind: downhill holes play directly into the prevailing wind while uphill holes play downwind. The delta between calm and windy winning scores here is roughly 10 strokes (-9 in windy 2025 vs. -20 in calm 2024). Players in the favorable Thursday wave could gain 2-3 strokes before hitting a shot.

keys.title: Driving distance is a trap. Bombers historically underperform at TPC San Antonio.
keys.description: Despite being the 5th-longest course on the 2026 schedule at 7,438 yards, raw distance provides almost no advantage. Recent winners averaged only 26th in driving distance. Three of the four par 5s are 591+ yards, but par-5 GIR is only 11% (vs. 22% Tour avg) and less than 45% of the field goes for the green in two. Conners won in 2023 ranking 51st in distance. Spieth won in 2021 as a below-average driver. The tight fairways and demanding approaches neutralize length and reward accuracy.

keys.title: Course familiarity is a massive edge. The 54-hole leader has won 10 of 15 times.
keys.description: TPC San Antonio rewards experience more than almost any course on Tour. The third-round leader or co-leader has gone on to win 10 of 15 times since the course became the Valero host in 2010. DataGolf identifies this as one of the courses where course history is most predictive. Jordan Spieth's SG per round at TPC San Antonio is the best of anyone in the field—"and it's not even close." He's made 9 of 10 cuts with 5 top-12 finishes and a victory. Denny McCarthy has 4 top-20s in 6 starts here. First-time visitors face a steep learning curve on the complex green complexes and wind patterns.

keys.title: The "Fantastic Finish" stretch (15-18) is where tournaments are won and lost.
keys.description: Holes 15 through 18 form the dramatic closing stretch. Hole 15 (464 yds, #2 HCP) is a brute par 4 that begins the gauntlet. Hole 16 (183 yds) is the signature par 3 with a center bunker splitting the green into three targets—Spieth aced it in 2024; Finau made a hole-in-one here in 2025. Hole 17 (347 yds) is a drivable par 4 with a 31% birdie rate where aggressive players make their move. Hole 18 (591 yds) is an uphill closer with a creek bisecting the fairway and a three-level green—eagle possible, bogey lurking. It's played over par every year since 2010.

prediction.summary: Jordan Spieth has the best course history in the field by a wide margin, and his approach play is peaking at exactly the right time. Si Woo Kim brings the single best stat-to-course fit in the field—2nd on Tour in SG: Approach at a course where approach is everything. Russell Henley is the analytical model's #1 pick with elite bogey avoidance and driving accuracy. The windy Thursday/Friday will thin the field early, and Spieth's 9-of-10 cut-making record here means he'll be there on the weekend. The calm Sunday conditions (59°F, 10 mph) will favor low closers.

phases.title: Thursday/Friday — Survival Mode
phases.description: The 20+ mph winds transform TPC San Antonio into one of the hardest courses on Tour. Scoring averages balloon to 73-74. The wave advantage is enormous—players with calmer tee times could gain 2-3 strokes. Spieth, who ranks 3rd in the field in wind performance, posts a steady 69 in the wind. Kim fires a 67 in the favorable wave. Several Masters hopefuls (Finau, Tom Kim) struggle in the gusts.

phases.title: Saturday — Moving Day as Cold Front Arrives
phases.description: The cold front drops temperatures from 88°F Friday to 75°F Saturday. Winds ease to 10 mph. Scoring opens up. Spieth fires a 65, climbing into the top 3 on the back of elite approach play. Henley's bogey-free Saturday puts him in contention. Fleetwood's short game keeps him alive despite approach struggles. The leaderboard takes shape for Sunday.

phases.title: Sunday — 59°F and Calm, Closers Strike
phases.description: The coolest final round in recent Valero history. Calm winds and firm greens reward precision. Spieth's familiarity with the Fantastic Finish stretch (15-18) proves decisive—he birdies the drivable 17th and pars the treacherous 18th to win by two. The Dallas native celebrates his second Valero title at the course that keeps giving him breakthroughs.

projectedScore.rationale: The windy Thursday/Friday will inflate scoring significantly, but the calm weekend will allow contenders to go low. Projected winning score of -14 splits the difference between the windy 2025 (-9) and calm 2024 (-20), reflecting two tough scoring days and two scorable ones.

tournamentPrediction.summary: Jordan Spieth has the best course history in the field by a wide margin—his SG per round at TPC San Antonio is "first in this field, and it's not even close." His approach play is peaking (+0.548 over his last 5 events), he ranks top 5 in this field in approach, around-the-green, and fairways hit, and he's a Dallas native with 9 of 10 cuts made here. Si Woo Kim brings the single best stat-to-course fit—2nd on Tour in SG: Approach at the approach course. Russell Henley is the analytical model's #1 pick. The windy Thursday/Friday will thin the field, and Spieth's wind performance (3rd in field) gives him an early edge. The calm, cool Sunday favors precision players who know every contour of these greens.

phases.title: Thursday/Friday — Survival Mode
phases.description: The 20+ mph winds transform TPC San Antonio into one of the hardest courses on Tour. Scoring averages balloon to 73-74. The wave advantage is enormous. Spieth, who ranks 3rd in wind performance, posts a steady 69 in the worst conditions. Kim fires a 67 in the favorable wave. Several Masters hopefuls—Finau, Tom Kim, Fowler—struggle in the gusts. The projected cut line is Even to +1.

phases.title: Saturday — Cold Front Moving Day
phases.description: The cold front drops temperatures from 88°F Friday to 75°F Saturday. Winds ease to 10 mph. Scoring opens up. Spieth fires a 65, climbing into the top 3 on elite approach play. Henley's bogey-free Saturday puts him in contention. Fleetwood's short game keeps him alive. The leaderboard takes shape for Sunday.

phases.title: Sunday — Cool and Calm
phases.description: At 59°F with 10 mph winds, the coolest final round in recent Valero history rewards precision. Spieth's familiarity with the Fantastic Finish stretch proves decisive—he birdies the drivable 17th and navigates 18's creek-bisected fairway for par. The Dallas native wins his second Valero title at the course that keeps giving him breakthroughs.

projectedWinner.name: Jordan Spieth
projectedWinner.rationale: Best course history in the field (won 2021, runner-up 2015, 5 top-12s in 10 starts). SG per round at TPC San Antonio leads the entire field "and it's not even close." Approach play is peaking at +0.548 over last 5 events. Dallas native with comfort and motivation at his home-state event. Wind performance (3rd in field) gives him an edge in Thursday/Friday conditions. The 54-hole leader has won 10 of 15 times here—if Spieth is near the lead Saturday night, history says he closes.

players.name: Jordan Spieth
players.stats: SG: T2G +2.3/round (1st in field) • SG: Approach +0.548 (last 5 events) • Top 5 in field in approach, ATG, & fairways
players.bio: The best course history in the field, and it's not even close. Spieth has made 9 of 10 cuts at TPC San Antonio with 5 top-12 finishes, a runner-up (2015), and a victory in 2021 that ended an 82-event, 1,351-day winless drought. His SG per round at this course leads the entire field. Born and raised in Dallas, the three-time major champion played college golf at UT Austin—this is his home state event. After years of injury struggles, he's pain-free and his iron play is peaking: +0.548 SG: Approach over his last 5 events with 3 top-12 finishes in his last 4 starts. He aced the signature Hole 16 here in 2024. The course that gave him one breakthrough may give him another.

players.name: Russell Henley
players.stats: Bogey avoidance: 9th on Tour (10.19%) • Driving accuracy: 5th on Tour • #1 in analytical model
players.bio: The analytical model's #1 pick for this week—and it's easy to see why. Henley's game is built for TPC San Antonio: 5th on Tour in driving accuracy for the narrow fairways, 9th in bogey avoidance for the monster par 4s, and solid approach play (+0.324 season). He finished 4th here last year and has been remarkably consistent in 2026 (T19-T8-T19-MC-T6-T13). At 296 yards, he's one of the shortest hitters in the field—but at a course where distance doesn't predict winners and accuracy does, that's an advantage. The kind of player who grinds out 68s while others oscillate between 65 and 73.

players.name: Si Woo Kim
players.stats: SG: Approach +1.198 (2nd on PGA Tour) • Gained 4+ strokes on approach in 3 of last 4 starts
players.bio: The best value in the field, according to multiple analysts. Si Woo Kim ranks 2nd on the entire PGA Tour in SG: Approach—the single most predictive stat at TPC San Antonio, where 7 of 8 recent winners ranked top 4 in approach during their winning week. He's gained 4+ strokes on approach in 3 of his last 4 events and says he's "swinging it the best he ever has." His course history backs it up: T4 (2019), T13 (2022), T23 (2021). The 2017 Players Championship winner is a three-time PGA Tour champion who thrives on technical, demanding courses. At 22/1, he may be the most overlooked contender in San Antonio.

players.name: Tommy Fleetwood
players.stats: SG: Around the Green +0.900 (1st on PGA Tour) • FedExCup champion • 3 top-10s in 2026
players.bio: The highest-ranked player in the field and the owner of the best short game on the PGA Tour—but there's a catch. Fleetwood's SG: Around the Green of +0.900 leads the entire Tour and is perfect for a course where only 58% of greens are hit in regulation. His scrambling ability could save strokes that others give back. But his season-long SG: Approach ranks just 97th (-0.048)—a major concern at the #1 approach-dependent course on Tour. Can the best short game compensate for inconsistent iron play over 72 holes? That's the Fleetwood paradox this week. The reigning FedExCup champion has 3 top-10s in 2026 but "can rack up top-10 finishes but refuses to win."

players.name: Denny McCarthy
players.stats: SG/round at TPC San Antonio: +1.82 (2nd in field) • Runner-up 2024 • 4 top-20s in 6 starts here
players.meta: Age 33 • World #92 • +4900 • 0 PGA Tour wins in 221 starts
players.bio: The ultimate course horse. Denny McCarthy's SG per round at TPC San Antonio is +1.82—second only to Jordan Spieth in this field. He's finished top 20 in 4 of his 6 starts here, including a heartbreaking runner-up in 2024 when he lost a playoff to Akshay Bhatia after firing a final-round 63. That 2024 finish set the tournament record at -20 (268). The reason he's a sleeper at 49/1: his approach play has surged recently, ranking 11th and 19th in SG: Approach in his last two events—his best approach stretch since August 2020. Still searching for PGA Tour win #1 despite $21.8M+ in career earnings. If there's a course where he breaks through, it's this one.

players.name: Sudarshan Yellamaraju
players.stats: SG: Approach +0.511 (23rd on Tour) • 8th in field on approach • T5 at Players, 6th at Houston
players.meta: Age 23 • Canadian (born in India) • PGA Tour rookie • +5000
players.bio: The breakout story of the 2026 PGA Tour season. Born in India, moved to Winnipeg at age 4, and taught himself to play golf by watching YouTube videos of Tiger Woods and Rory McIlroy with his father. He still doesn't have a swing coach—when he needs help, he sends videos to his dad. This unlikely path led to a T5 at The Players Championship and a 6th-place finish at the Houston Open last week. He ranks 8th in this field in SG: Approach—the #1 predictive stat at TPC San Antonio. At 315 yards off the tee (16th on Tour), he has the length to handle the 7,438-yard course. Multiple analysts say the market "hasn't fully captured how good Sudarshan is yet." At 50/1, he's the clear value play.

players.name: Rickie Fowler
players.stats: World #65 • Must win for Masters invite • Former Masters runner-up
players.meta: Age 37 • +6600 • No other path to Augusta
players.bio: The stakes could not be higher. Rickie Fowler must win the Valero Texas Open to earn a Masters invitation—there is no other path to Augusta. The fan favorite and former Masters runner-up has watched his world ranking plummet from the top 10 to #65, and he's in danger of missing the Masters for the fourth time in five years. He missed the cut at the Houston Open last week. The odds are long at 66/1, but Fowler has always been a player who rises to the occasion when the narrative demands it. His last top-10 at TPC San Antonio came in 2018 (T6). If he catches fire in the favorable weather wave, the crowd will carry him.

players.name: Alex Noren
players.stats: SG: ATG 9th on Tour • #4 in analytical model • T15 (2023), T14 (2024) at TPC San Antonio
players.bio: The model darling this week. Alex Noren ranks 4th in the GolfNewsNet analytical model despite 35/1 odds—a significant gap between his statistical profile and the betting market. The Swedish veteran has two consecutive top-15 finishes at TPC San Antonio (T15 in 2023, T14 in 2024) and ranks 9th on Tour in SG: Around the Green. He's a proven performer in windy Texas conditions, with a runner-up at Port Royal, 3rd at TPC Craig Ranch, and 4th at Memorial Park. His accurate driving suits the narrow fairways. At 43, he's the quintessential "boring but effective" pick—the kind of player who grinds out four rounds of 68-69 while flashier names make bogeys.

funFacts.title: Third-Oldest Event on Tour
funFacts.body: The Texas Open was first played in 1922—making it the third-oldest event on the PGA Tour behind only the Western Open (1899) and the Canadian Open (1904). It's the longest-running PGA Tour event held in the same city (San Antonio for 104 years).

funFacts.title: Arnold Palmer's Three-Peat
funFacts.body: Arnold Palmer won three consecutive Texas Opens from 1960 to 1962. The only other player to win three is Justin Leonard (2000, 2001, 2007). The King loved San Antonio.

funFacts.title: Kevin Na's Infamous 16
funFacts.body: In 2011, Kevin Na made a 16 on the par-4 9th hole—the only hole on the course without bunkers. He hit the ball back and forth in the native brush, eventually finishing with one of the highest scores in PGA Tour history on a single hole.

funFacts.title: Spieth's Ace on 16
funFacts.body: Jordan Spieth made a hole-in-one on the signature 16th hole during the 2024 Valero Texas Open. Tony Finau did the same in 2025 from 199 yards to vault inside the cut line. The center-bunker par 3 is arguably one of the most unique in golf.

funFacts.title: $228 Million for Charity
funFacts.body: The Valero Texas Open has raised over $228 million for charity since Valero Energy became the title sponsor in 2002. It's the annual leader in charitable fundraising among all PGA Tour events. Valero Energy is headquartered in San Antonio.

funFacts.title: The 10-Stroke Wind Tax
funFacts.body: The difference between windy and calm winning scores at TPC San Antonio is staggering. Brian Harman won at -9 in windy 2025 conditions. Akshay Bhatia won at -20 in calm 2024 conditions. That's an 11-stroke swing purely from weather.

funFacts.title: First PGA Tour 60 Ever
funFacts.body: Al Brosch shot 60 at the 1951 Texas Open at Brackenridge Park—the first 60 in PGA Tour history. The Texas Open has always been ahead of the scoring curve.

funFacts.title: Monday Qualifier to Champion
funFacts.body: Corey Conners won the 2019 Valero Texas Open after Monday qualifying into the field—the first player to do so in nine years. He then went on to win it again in 2023.

funFacts.title: The Wind Design Philosophy
funFacts.body: Greg Norman designed TPC San Antonio so that all downhill holes play into the prevailing wind and all uphill holes play downwind. This creates a natural rhythm where the course alternately squeezes and releases pressure. In 2015, the morning/afternoon wave scoring differential was 3.9 strokes—the largest on Tour.

funFacts.title: Yellamaraju's YouTube Golf School
funFacts.body: PGA Tour rookie Sudarshan Yellamaraju taught himself to play golf by watching YouTube videos of Tiger Woods and Rory McIlroy with his father in Winnipeg. He still doesn't have a swing coach. He ranked T5 at The Players Championship and ranks 8th in this field in SG: Approach—at 50/1 odds.

funFacts.title: McCarthy's 221-Start Drought
funFacts.body: Denny McCarthy has earned $21.8 million in his PGA Tour career across 221 starts without a single victory. He lost a heartbreaking playoff at this very tournament in 2024. His SG per round at TPC San Antonio is the second-best in the field.

funFacts.title: The Signature 16th
funFacts.body: The par-3 16th features a center bunker that splits the green into three distinct targets. Players must commit to a side before the tee shot—there's no safe middle option. It's been called "arguably one of the most unique par 3s in golf."

history.callout: The Valero Texas Open is one of the oldest and most storied events on the PGA Tour, first played in 1922 as the Texas Open. It moved to TPC San Antonio's Oaks Course in 2010, where accuracy and iron play—not distance—decide champions. Three of the last six winners used this tournament to punch their Masters ticket. The course has produced first-time winners (Spaun 2022, Landry 2018), drought-breakers (Spieth 2021), and dramatic finishes (Bhatia-McCarthy playoff 2024).

subsections.title: TPC San Antonio Era: 2010-Present
subsections.content: Since moving to TPC San Antonio, the Valero Texas Open has rewarded precision ball-strikers and produced some of golf's best storylines.

timeline.text: Adam Scott wins the inaugural event at TPC San Antonio at -14, christening the Greg Norman-designed Oaks Course.
Kevin Na makes a 16 on the par-4 9th hole—one of the highest single-hole scores in PGA Tour history. Brendan Steele wins at -8.
Corey Conners Monday-qualifies into the field and wins at -20—the first Monday qualifier to win on Tour in nine years. Earns his first Masters invitation.
Jordan Spieth wins at -18, ending an 82-event, 1,351-day winless drought. The Dallas native's emotional victory launches his comeback.
J.J. Spaun wins his first Tour title in his 147th start at -13, surviving a double-bogey on the 1st hole in the final round.
Akshay Bhatia beats Denny McCarthy in a playoff at -20, tying the TPC San Antonio record. Both earned Masters invitations via their finishes.
Brian Harman wins at just -9 in brutal wind conditions—the toughest-scoring Valero in the TPC San Antonio era.

subsections.title: Legends of the Texas Open
subsections.content: The Texas Open's winner list spans a century of golf greatness. Walter Hagen won in 1923. Byron Nelson in 1940. Ben Hogan in 1946. Sam Snead won twice (1948, 1950). Arnold Palmer won three straight (1960-62). Lee Trevino in 1980. Ben Crenshaw, the beloved Texan, won twice. Justin Leonard matched Palmer with three wins (2000, 2001, 2007). In 2003, Tommy Armour III shot 254 (-26)—a PGA Tour 72-hole record at the time—winning by seven strokes at La Cantera. The Texas Open has always been where future legends announce themselves and where established champions return to the well.

footerText: Valero Texas Open 2026 • TPC San Antonio (Oaks Course) • San Antonio, TX • Par 72, 7,438 yards • $9.8M purse • Golf Channel Thu-Fri 4-7 PM ET • NBC Sat-Sun
